title: Part 8
text: other 17 The person in question must be Durkheim’s contemporary, Camille Jullian (1859–1933, born like Durkheim on April 15). As a historian, philologist and archeologist, Jullian was well-positioned to appreciate the outstanding quality of van Gennep’s works, including those parts of it that touched upon Jullian’s field of expertise, namely antiquity. Whereas van Gennep insisted on liberating ethnography from history, he never showed real history that disdain which would become so marked among Durkheim and his followers. Jullian was a friend of Bergson, often identified as a ‘Liberal’, and just like van Gennep he nurtured a passion for local French history (he wrote the book on the history of Bordeaux still widely used today) and, at the general level, the intertwining of material and spiritual culture. He was Professor at the Collège de France from 1905. But in social situations he was also known to be shy and with a feeble voice, never imposing himself – more or less the opposite of Durkheim. It should also be mentioned that Durkheim had had his struggles with Jullian before. In 1904, when Gabriel Tarde died and Bergson obtained a Chair in Social Philosophy, Durkheim and his collaborators tried hard to transform Bergson’s former chair in Greek and Latin philosophy into a chair in sociology – for which Mauss could then run as a candidate. However, they were voted down, and it was Jullian who was then awarded the chair in ‘History and National Treasures’. Jullian would support Mauss’ candidature in 1907 before that of van Gennep; but by 1915 van Gennep’s merits were so substantial that it had become close to impossible to vote against his candidature. 18 The irony of the irony is this: had van Gennep nurtured German sympathies, it would have done him little harm in Switzerland – perhaps quite the contrary. Instead he publicly denounced the Swiss government for their pro-German attitude via several public letters to the daily, Dépeche de Toulouse. And for that he was forced to return Liminality and the Modern 68 members of the committee, did not consider his works of any great value. His candidature was turned down. It seems that van Gennep was never again taken up in the correspondence between Durkheim and Mauss. It must be stressed that while we may lack further direct proof of Durkheim’s role in preventing van Gennep’s access to the job market, the situation must be put in its general context. Durkheim had held a chair’s position at the Sorbonne since 1902, but he had also built up a much wider power base. Some of Durkheim’s enemies would openly accuse him of ‘managing’ appointments and creating chairs of sociology in provincial universities in order to extend his influence (see Jones 1986: 12–23). Leaving aside the various aspects of the situation that will probably remain conjectural, the fact remains that one of France’s most promising social scientists did not even manage to land a teaching position at a minor provincial university. And so, with 20 languages spoken and twice the number read, a handful of the most important social science books translated from English into French, with twelve books published together with around 100 articles and reviews, directing a fairly successful journal of ethnography and sociology, with administrative skills from a position within a French ministry, with practical skills in museology, with fieldwork experience in Algeria and sustained ethnographic data-gathering in several French provinces, having just recently, as the first person ever, assembled 600 of Europe’s most promising names in ethnography and anthropology to discuss the raison d’être and future of their discipline, leading what would for decades remain the biggest anthropological conference ever held, by the end of 1915 Arnold van Gennep would return to France empty-handed. And unemployed. On the Parallels between Gabriel Tarde and Arnold Van Gennep To suggest strong parallels between the work of Arnold van Gennep and Gabriel Tarde may seem both unnecessary, forced and anachronistic. And in many ways, they are of course not comparable. Tarde and van Gennep were not close friends or allies. They belonged to different generations and only lived in Paris at the same time for a few years, from 1901 when van Gennep returned from Poland to Paris until 1904 when Tarde died – and even then they apparently did not visit the same circles. Gabriel Tarde had a wildly successful career, crowned in 1900 with the Chair of modern philosophy at the Collège de France (much to Durkheim’s anger); he was 15 years older than Durkheim. Arnold van Gennep was instead 15 years younger than Durkheim, had a different background, was interested in different areas of research than those of Tarde, and never even held a position in France. And yet the parallels are strikingly strong, and for four reasons that we can quickly spell out (for further detail, see Thomassen 2012 c). The purpose is, again, not to his homeland France – where he would once again be put under suspicion for being potentially anti-patriotic. Arnold van Gennep and his Contemporaries 69 just a matter of intellectual history: what is at stake is an attempt to re-establish foundations from which to analyse social phenomena in ways that are meaningful. First, and most evidently, even if Tarde had a successful career, he was eventually sidelined by the Durkheimian school, and in a way even more radically so than Arnold van Gennep, who after all ‘survived’ as a folklorist. This has much to do with the fact that Tarde committed the unforgivable error of dying in 1904, just as Durkheim was about to secure his stronghold. Tarde’s writings were dismissed and literally taken out of the sociological curriculum. For decades – in fact, for most of a century – they only survived in America, via the Chicago school of sociology. Second, what unites Tarde and van Gennep is that they were unlucky enough to hold knowledge and methodological expertise in exactly those areas that Durkheim was trying to claim ‘his own’, and which he programmatically used to establish sociology as an empirically founded science: state statistics on suicide and ethnography of primitive religion. Even here the very same mediating role of Mauss in the data-gathering is striking: Mauss had learned much about aboriginal religion and totemism by studying it with van Gennep (and by reading his work); ten years earlier it was again Mauss who directed himself to Tarde who as ‘Director of the Bureau of Legal Statistics’ would grant Mauss and Durkheim access to the statistical material that made up the entire empirical dataset used for Suicide. Third, and as already hinted at, in terms of methodological approach, van Gennep and Tarde come quite close to each other. This also concerns the relationship to neighbouring disciplines of ethnography and sociology. Durkheim’s own position is ambivalent: on the one hand he wants to borrow and implement the scientific method from the natural sciences; on the other hand he wants sociology to cut its ties completely from biology and physics. Tarde’s position is the reverse (Latour 2010: 3): ‘for him there exist only societies. Human societies are but a particular subset of these societies because they exist in so few copies. But since human societies are accessible through their most intimate features, social scientists have no need to let natural scientists dictate what their epistemology should be’. As Tarde put it himself (1903: 1): ‘In social subjects we are exceptionally privileged in having veritable causes, positive and specific acts; this condition is wholly lacking in every other subject of investigation’. This coheres perfectly with van Gennep’s approach. Van Gennep insists on the irreducibility of social phenomena, but at the same time he refuses to cut off the social sciences from what he at times called the ‘great tree of life’. 19 Just like Tarde, van Gennep based 19 And here again, on this crucial point of methodology, Mauss would move closer to both Tarde and van Gennep after Durkheim’s death, which is most visible in his 1927 discussion of the new divisions of the social sciences; here Mauss pays lip service to Durkheim’s categorical divisions, then slowly but surely moves beyond them in both form and substance, and ends on the following statement: ‘Normally the greatest advances occur on the borders between the division of sociology, as on the borders between all sciences and parts of sciences’ (Mauss 2005[1927]: 65, my emphasis). Liminality and the Modern 70 his interpretations on the assumption that man is a part of nature and is therefore subject to the great natural laws of stability, variability, and change. Fourth, both Tarde and van Gennep concurred that Durkheim and his followers simply failed to capture exactly what they themselves argued that sociology and anthropology should be all about, namely the collective level and ‘collective representations’. Where do such representations come from? How are they established? How are they passed on? How do they interrelate with the individual? The parallels between the Tarde/Durkheim and the Durkheim/Arnold van Gennep debates are both strong and important here. Both thinkers were equally aware of the problematic dismissal of historical individuals as agents of society and social change. Both Gabriel Tarde and Arnold van Gennep saw the extent to which it was Durkheim himself, against all his proclamations, who had turned society into a ‘metaphysical entity’. 20 While the closeness of these parallels may be open to further discussion, the real point to stress in this context does not simply belong to intellectual history. The central concept that Tarde engaged was that of imitation. In Chapter 4 of Suicide Durkheim violently and categorically dismissed the relevance of this concept for the social sciences (see again Thomassen 2012d). 21 Even though Durkheim’s argument is an evident absurdity (have we ever lived in a more imitative age?), this was also a debate he ‘won’. No introductory books on sociology list imitation as a key concept even today. Likewise, van Gennep’s recognition of the centrality of liminality is only now, with a century of delay, starting to reach us. Liminality and imitation belong together in our attempt to formulate an analytical platform for approaching change and transition and human reactions to in-between periods and situations. However, this exercise must pay due attention to the work of an anthropologist who did more than any other social scientist to re-launch Arnold van Gennep and the concept of liminality: Victor Turner. 20 Incidentally, Durkheim, even during his studies at the École Normale, was soon nicknamed ‘the metaphysician’ by his peers. 21 Tarde wrote a prompt answer to Durkheim in which he rebuts the worst of Durkheim’s claims, but for reasons we can only guess at chose not to publish them. Tarde’s hand-written notes were recently transcribed and published by Besnard and Berlandi (Tarde 2000). Chapter 3 Liminality Rediscovered: With Victor Turner and Beyond This chapter sets itself a simple task: to trace the reception of Van Gennep’s ideas and how they were developed in the latter part of the twentieth century and up until today, with a focus on the concept of liminality. This is where we have to go in some detail into the work of Victor Turner, and for obvious reasons: it was via the work of Turner that liminality re-entered anthropology and the social sciences. Both the merits and limits of Turner’s work will be discussed. Arnold van Gennep: Delayed Reception and Posthumous Recognition Van Gennep became known in Anglo-Saxon anthropology after 1960 with the translation of Rites of Passage. This happened in the context of British anthropologists’ renewed interests in theoretical developments within French anthropology. Rodney Needham and Edmund Leach, supported by Evans-Pritchard, took the initiative to translate the most important classics of French anthropologists, like Marcel Mauss, Robert Hertz, Hubert and Mauss, and indeed also of some of Durkheim’s less well-known works. It was also Needham who (somewhat surprisingly perhaps, given the marginality of the book within van Gennep’s own oeuvre) translated and introduced van Gennep’s otherwise little known The Semi-Scholars in 1967. The book is interesting for a number of reasons beyond our present discussion. The book is composed of a series of small stories and must be read as van Gennep’s sharp, humoristic and ironic denouement of French academic life. It has barely received attention within France. The general interest in French ethnology was very much due to the high status that Claude Lévi-Strauss and his structuralist approach had attained. Lévi-Strauss had himself recognized the value of van Gennep’s early work in his book on totemism, remarking upon van Gennep’s ‘unusual theoretical boldness’ (Lévi- Strauss 1963: 4). Anglo-Saxon readers must have noticed with some curiosity how Lévi-Strauss sets out his own discussion with reference to Arnold van Gennep – and not to Durkheim. As we saw, it was indeed van Gennep who had translated Frazer’s book on totemism into French. Lévi-Strauss simply had to recognize his expertise and the potential of his analytical approach, clearly seeing van Gennep as a better starting point than Durkheim. Edmund Leach thought likewise. In a 1968 overview essay on ritual, Leach bluntly stated that ‘van Gennep’s schema has proved more useful than Durkheim’s’ (1968: 522). This was new. Liminality and the Modern 72 Leach’s recognition of van Gennep’s superiority to Durkheim is far from strange, as Leach had himself paid great attention to the ‘rhythmical’, seasonal and ritualistic changes of roles and even kinship terms and names in his own ethnographic work (Leach 1954). Lévi-Strauss’ (albeit partial) recognition of van Gennep is also not strange, for van Gennep always insisted that ceremonial patterns should be examined as wholes and that comparison should be based upon similarities in structure rather than upon content. Because van Gennep never became established in French academic life, he also by and large failed to have an impact outside France. He did become an important figure in Swiss, and of course French, folklore. However, the discipline of folklore was never something with a large reach. In France it was not even a university discipline until 1943. From the early 1920s, van Gennep was mostly working on his own, building up a discipline which had no official scientific status, no curriculum, and therefore no students. That was his destiny, but he coped with it and kept on working as long as he lived. This almost total lack of recognition meant that in post-Durkheimian anthropology, the concept of liminality was entirely absent. Besides Henri Junod, one of the only anthropologists of importance to discuss Rites of Passage before its translation in 1960 was Paul Radin in his Primitive Religion (1937). Radin positively referred to van Gennep’s work on totemism (pp. 203–4), and Chapter 5 of Primitive Religion, on The Crisis of Life and Transition Rites, was quite simply an application of van Gennep’s framework. 1 Radin also anticipated some of Turner’s ideas: Chapter 14 was on ‘ritual drama’ (the title of the chapter). This is a significant exception, for as we shall argue below, Radin’s work on the trickster greatly complements the analytical vigour of liminality as tied to crisis situations. However, this application was more like a single rupture in a sea of amnesia. The general fact is that van Gennep became known to Anglo-Saxon anthropology only with the 1960 translation of his book. He was not on anyone’s reading list before that; and in that very concrete sense, as happened with Tarde, Durkheim’s triumph had been total. The English Translation of Rites of Passage The English version of Rites of Passage today exists in five different editions, those of 1960, 1977, 1980, 2004 and, most recently, 2010 (a reprint of the 1977 edition). The 1960 edition by Routledge and Kegan Paul, printed by the Chicago University Press in America, was the decisive one. It quite suddenly made 1 In Turner’s own brief overview of van Gennep’s reception (1985: 158–9), he mentions the work of E. D. Chappell and C. S. Cohn, who in their 1942 Principles of Anthropology discussed rites of passage; drawing on Kimball, Turner also makes mention of scholarship in psychology which had applied van Gennep’s scheme toward the analysis of individual development. For the linkage between liminality and contemporary affectivity literature, see Thomassen (2013c). Liminality Rediscovered: With Victor Turner and Beyond 73 Arnold van Gennep known in Anglo-Saxon academia. The reviews were almost overwhelming. His original book from 1909 had in fact been reviewed quite positively in both English and American journals, but everyone had forgotten about that. If anyone remembered anything, then it was Mauss’ dismissive review. The book had drowned in oblivion, and van Gennep’s name was simply not known. Only a very few anthropologists in the Anglo-Saxon world had made reference to the book – indeed very few anthropologists and sociologists as such had made reference to the book. Now everything changed. Unfortunately Arnold van Gennep did not live to see this belated reception of his book: he had died three years earlier, on 8 May 1957, at the age of 84. Rites of Passage is one of the books that since then has been quoted in the greatest variety of fields and disciplines; it is a fame that van Gennep knew nothing of while he was alive, living like a ‘hermit’. Outside academia the very concept, rite of passage, has become a household term in the entire English-speaking world – and this is a rare feat for an indeed highly academic book. As a book, Rites of Passage is in fact slightly difficult to approach for non-specialists. It is meticulously empirical and makes reference to a vast literature written in more than a dozen languages, and more than a century ago by now. Some of Van Gennep’s comparisons are made between tribal groups or peoples that were already near extinction at the moment of publication. Yet without a doubt this is one of the most successful books ever published in anthropology. And still the publication – and the larger reception – has its serious pitfalls. The initiative for the 1960 translation and publication was taken by Solon T. Kimball. The translation of the book (the same translation used in all later editions by Routledge and Chicago) was done by Monika B. Wizedom and Gabrielle L. Caffee, supervised by Kimball himself. It is a good translation. In its passage to the Anglo-Saxon world the book got itself a new subtitle. The French version had been published by É. Nourry 2 (Paris) as Les Rites de passage, étude systématique des rites de la porte et du seuil, de l’hospitalité, de l’adoption, de la grossesse et de l’accouchement, de la naissance, de l’enfance, de la puberté, de l’initiation, de l’ordination, du couronnement, des fiançailles et du mariage, des funérailles, des saisons, etc. Books published in that period often had extremely long and explanatory subtitles, sometimes close to complete outlines of the actual content, and quite typically ending with an ‘etc.’. Not surprisingly, in French- speaking environments, the book has most often been abbreviated as Les Rites de passage, étude systématique, or simply Rites de Passage. The University of Chicago Press together with Kimball chose to give the book the title we are now familiar with: The Rites of Passage. A classic study of cultural 2 Nourry was van Gennep’s friend, who himself wrote about folklore under a pseudonym (De Saintyves). Nourry had helped van Gennep in the preparation of the book. Van Gennep therefore ends his foreword with these cryptic words: ‘Thus the publisher has been the victim of the scholar and friend within him. I hope, at least, that he will not be a victim of the reader’ (1960: XXVI). Liminality and the Modern 74 celebrations. There might have been some discussion on how to translate ‘rites de passage’. An alternative could have been ‘ritual passages’, which is probably the English term that most people would use today. But ‘rites of passage’ stays closer to the French original. The subtitle was altogether new. In a way it remains a dubious subtitle: ‘cultural celebrations’ is a rather vague term, and actually not one used by Van Gennep, who rather spoke of ‘ceremonies’ – so this would have been a more correct word. 3 On the other hand, the publishers were surely right on target about the term ‘classic’, although they could not possibly have known that with any certainty in 1960. The book had not even remotely established as a ‘classic’ within French anthropology. It had, after all, been written more than 50 years earlier, by an author nobody really knew, and the book had made almost no impact. But Kimball was sure that this would change with the new publication in English. And here he was right. A forgotten book did become a classic with half a century of delay. So far so good. Rites of Passage was reprinted in 1965. The later editions, from 1977, 1980, 2004 and 2010 are hardly worth mentioning, except for the fact that these are the editions that most students use today. The Routledge paperback version of The Rites of Passage that appeared in 1977 surprisingly dropped Kimball’s Introduction, although it still figured on the title page and in the table of contents. Amazingly enough, this error has not been rectified since: in all later editions, Kimball’s introduction still figures in the table of contents, but it is not there for anyone to read. Moreover, and more seriously, no alternative introduction was written. All the reader gets as context is Arnold van Gennep’s own foreword finished in December 1908, with implicit references that no contemporary reader will be able to decipher. It is not much of a help for new readers encountering van Gennep with a century of delay. On the one hand, one could argue that a thorough introduction is no longer necessary, as the book itself has by now become famous. On the other hand, the lack of a proper introduction to such an important book must be seen as yet another symptom of the continuous neglect of Arnold van Gennep’s work. The perception that this book ‘speaks for itself’ is deeply erroneous. If any, this is a book that requires a proper framing. More nuanced introductions to the book have been written for other translations (see for example the Introduction by Remotti written for the 1981 Italian translation). The book stands in desperate need of a new introduction and proper contextualization in the English translation. As it is, the Introduction to the book written by Kimball is the only one ever written in English; next to shorter Encyclopaedia entries, it is probably also the most read introduction to Arnold van Gennep’s work as such. This, it must be said, is rather unfortunate. In his Introduction, Kimball identified van Gennep as a member of a generation of French sociologists inspired by positivism, and suggested that van Gennep be seen as an example of French scholars like Mauss, 3 In the 1969 French re-edition, which included van Gennep’s own notes to the first publication, van Gennep himself indicates that it might have been better to replace ‘rites’ with ‘ceremonies’. Liminality Rediscovered: With Victor Turner and Beyond 75 Hubert and Durkheim who were collectively developing a functionalist approach (Kimball 1960: vii). This contextualization of van Gennep’s work is both right and wrong and for reasons already explained: van Gennep was indeed involved in developing an entire ‘approach’, but it was not really ‘functionalist’ in the sense that readers in 1960 would understand the term, and it happened in a struggle against the Durkheimians, not simply as a ‘member’ of that Durkheimian generation. Kimball only slowly started to find out about this struggle after 1960, as it had been hidden away from sight, even within France. Therefore, while the publication of the book in English made van Gennep a ‘new classic’ with half a century of delay, the Introduction certainly did not facilitate a proper reception, for it bypassed how and why van Gennep had been ostracized by the academic world, and how his theoretical project differed from that of Durkheim and his followers. The Introduction simply fails to place the book within van Gennep’s academic and intellectual trajectory. After his death, Arnold van Gennep was ‘rediscovered’ in several stages. One of the most prominent thinkers to recognize his importance was Claude Lévi-Strauss. As noted above, van Gennep paid great attention to systems of exchange, and may have inspired both Mauss and Lévi-Strauss more than has so far been recognized, and more than both Mauss and Lévi-Strauss themselves ever conceded. In this vein, Senn has argued that van Gennep be recognized as a precursor to structuralism (Senn 1974: 242). However, Lévi-Strauss’ ‘rediscovery’ of van Gennep remained constrained by his structuralist approach and search for laws of logic. This was certainly miles away from van Gennep’s own attempt to establish a ‘biological sociology’, a science studying faits naissants, i.e. cultural phenomena at their moment of occurrence. Lévi-Straussian structuralism used finished texts (myths, kinship terminologies, cooking recipes) as its data. Liminality makes sense only within social dramas as they unfold. Despite their recognition of van Gennep, it is therefore not surprising that the concept of liminality was not at all taken up by Lévi-Strauss or any other member of the French or British structuralist schools, even as they slowly started to recognize van Gennep’s brilliance. It was Victor Turner who re-discovered the importance of liminality. It was one of Turner’s many merits to ‘liberate’ van Gennep’s framework from both the functionalist and structuralist straight-jackets, inserting van Gennep’s book on ritual passages where it truly belongs: in a processual approach. During his fieldwork, Turner had heard about van Gennep via the work of Henri Junod (Turner 1985: 159). However, his real encounter with van Gennep happened in 1963, and it conducted him toward his lifelong exploration into the liminal. We need now to engage with those explorations, in order to understand how, via the work of Turner, liminality made its way back into anthropology and the social sciences. Liminality and the Modern 76 Turner’s Encounter with Arnold van Gennep: From Social Dramas to Liminality Victor Turner (1920–1984) is without doubt one of the most fascinating and important anthropologists of the twentieth century. He always had an acute perception of the importance of threshold experiences. It is difficult not to think of a certain parallel with Arnold van Gennep in terms of formative life experiences. Van Gennep, we remember, lost his father after a sudden divorce and was then taken by his mother to live in another country at the age of six. More or less the same happened to Turner. Turner was born in Scotland, but at the age of eleven he was suddenly forced to leave as he went with his divorced mother to live with his maternal grandparents in Bournemouth, England. Turner later studied English language and literature at University College London (1938–1941). During the war he spent some time in a public library, and had a formative reading experience when he encountered Margaret Mead’s Coming of Age in Samoa. That famous book, as is well-known, describes the culture-specific ways in which children of either sex turn into adults. It was one of many attempts of the period to combine psychological perspectives concerning human personality with social analysis. Turner decided to study anthropology at University College of London. Here he attended lectures by some of Britain’s best anthropologists, including Daryll Forde, Meyer Fortes and Edmund Leach. He received his B.A. with honours in 1949. Max Gluckman, the leading member of the Manchester School, then offered Turner a grant from the Rhodes-Livingstone Institute to carry out fieldwork in an African tribe. Turner accepted and was assigned to the Mambwe tribe. In the event, he never reached the Mambwe homeland; during his stay at the Institute in Lusaka he received a telegram from Gluckman: ‘Suggest you change to Ndembu tribe Northwestern Province much malaria yellow fever plenty of ritual’ (E. Turner 1985: 2). In 1950, together with his wife Edith, Turner moved to the Mukanza village in the Mwinilunga district of Northern Rhodesia (now Zambia). Here Victor Turner started his fieldwork among the Ndembu, the ethnographic ground of his life’s work. Plenty of ritual indeed! Edith Turner was always with him, and it is well-known that she helped him greatly with data-gathering and its analysis. Turner had been trained in Marxist conflict theory and Durkheimian-inspired functionalist anthropology, and his early work largely stayed within this tradition, analysing schism and conflict as part of the social structure. The first result of Turner’s research among the Ndembu was his doctoral dissertation, later published as Schism and Continuity in an African Society: A Study of Ndembu Village Life (Turner 1957a). In this work, Turner analysed the mechanisms of resolving social conflicts in Ndembu society. The ritual theme was present, but very modestly so. Turner devoted only one of twelve chapters to the study of ritual, and it was indicatively entitled The Politically Integrative Function of Ritual. At this stage of his life, Turner saw rituals, just as he had been taught, as redressive mechanisms for the tensions produced in the secular order. Liminality Rediscovered: With Victor Turner and Beyond 77 Turner’s departure from this functionalist paradigm happened in steps, and the first such step was his introduction of the term, ‘social drama’. Turner had introduced this term because he sensed that much of social life was clearly theatrical. Anthropology therefore needed terms that would allow for a representation of culture in a more congruent way, and ‘social drama’ served this purpose well. It was also an attempt to take seriously the neo-Marxist interest in social conflict and functionalist focus on social integration, without actually employing the existing analytical vocabulary of those dominant paradigms. The social dramas among the Ndembu, Turner said, exhibit a processual form, following a pattern of four phases: (1) a breach of regular norm-governed social relationships between persons or groups of a social unit; (2) a crisis or extension of the breach, unless the conflict can be sealed off quickly; (3) adjustive and redressive mechanisms brought into operation by leading
